findings.problem: The chapter stages Havelock Pigments as a debt Gideon settles now: Silas receives a receipt marked settled in full, Gideon says he has purchased Silas time, and Junie treats Havelock's changed behavior that morning as new. The continuity sheet canon says the 43-crown Havelock debt was already cleared with Gideon's chapter 3 advance, so this opening bargain replays a resolved leverage point and breaks causal continuity.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_07.md:17, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_07.md:21, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_07.md:141 so the leverage object matches canon. Either make this receipt explicitly old proof of the chapter-3 settlement that Gideon is now weaponizing, or replace Havelock here with a different still-open creditor and adjust Junie's reaction and Silas's relief accordingly.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if a reader can compare the revised chapter with the continuity sheet and find one consistent status for the 43-crown Havelock debt: already paid before chapter 7, or absent from this scene because another creditor now fills that role. Fail if the revised lines still imply Gideon newly settles Havelock in chapter 7.

findings.problem: The chapter leans on the same "By the time..." temporal hinge at least six times. That repeated transition scaffold becomes audible as a tic in the very sections that should feel most alive to changing pressure: Silas leaving Gideon, the decoy-hunt montage, the moment the stolen names hit paper, the proofing-room theft, and the overnight posting run.
findings.rewrite_direction: Recast the transition beats at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_07.md:135, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_07.md:191, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_07.md:195, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_07.md:291, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_07.md:336, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_07.md:374 so they enter new time or consequence through varied syntax: sensory-first arrival, action-first cut, consequence-first statement, or dialogue interruption. Keep no more than two "By the time..." launches in the full chapter.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, on reread, no more than two of the cited transition beats still begin with "By the time" and each revised beat enters its moment with a distinct sentence shape. Fail if three or more of those cited beats still use the same temporal opener.

summary: Booker and dialogue are at pass level: the chapter hits its concealment beats, keeps Silas morally complicit without false nobility, and preserves distinct registers for Silas, Gideon, Junie, Inez, and Vero. It fails craft on a high continuity contradiction around the Havelock debt and fails prose on a repeated transition formula that starts to flatten the chapter's movement.
